channel.title: World news | The Guardian
channel.description: Latest World news news, comment and analysis from the Guardian, the world's leading liberal voice
channel.copyright: Guardian News and Media Limited or its affiliated companies. All rights reserved. 2026
channel.dc:rights: Guardian News and Media Limited or its affiliated companies. All rights reserved. 2026

item.title: Health worker monitored for Ebola in London hospital after working in DRC
item.description:
UK resident was evacuated from Democratic Republic of the Congo where there is an outbreak of the disease

A humanitarian worker is being monitored at a specialist hospital in London after potential exposure to the Ebola virus.

The UK resident was medically evacuated from the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) where the worker had been treating patients with the disease, the UK Health Security Agency (UKHSA) said.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Nadeem Badshah

item.title: Healthy diet too expensive for one in three people globally, UN report finds
item.description:
Global hunger declined for the third year in a row in 2025 but experts call for action to bring down costs of fruit, vegetables and dairy as report shows 2.69bn people can’t afford to eat well

One in three people cannot afford to eat a healthy diet, a new report has found, prompting calls for urgent action on food affordability.

There are 2.69 billion people unable to afford a diverse diet that meets their energy and nutrient needs, and it will take “immense” amounts of work to achieve the global target of zero hunger by 2030, the annual report from five UN agencies warned.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Kat Lay Global health correspondent

item.title: São Tomé and Príncipe heads to polls in tense presidential election
item.description:
Incumbent Carlos Vila Nova hopes to defeat his former party and secure second term as independent

Voters in São Tomé and Príncipe go to the polls for a presidential election on Sunday as one of Africa’s least populous countries seeks to burnish its democratic credentials.

According to the National Election Commission, about 142,000 people are registered to vote in the tiny African state’s elections, approximately 15% of whom live in the diaspora.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Eromo Egbejule in Abidjan

item.title: ‘We are preserving a tradition’: how Ghana’s sensationalist film posters became collectible art
item.description:
Hand-painted works are often wildly unfaithful to the movies they portray – reinterpretations that sometimes resulted in threats, insults and even physical attacks from viewers who felt duped

Sitting on his porch in Teshie near Accra, Heavy J dipped a brush into red oil paint and dabbed it carefully on to his canvas – a flour sack – adding blood to a knife being wielded by a man. Higher on the canvas, he had started on an outline of a skull.

Heavy J was creating a poster, but not as you might have expected for a horror film. Instead, it was for the animated fairytale The Little Mermaid. The man with the knife was not a killer but the film’s kind-hearted prince, Eric. The skull was also unrelated to the story. “We add more to make people interested,” said Heavy J, whose real name is Jeaurs Affutu.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Carlos Mureithi in Greater Accra

item.title: Seven Americans quarantining at Kenya Ebola facility after US travel ban, says aid group
item.description:
Aid workers are first known people to quarantine at facility, which sparked huge opposition in Kenya

Seven American aid workers who had been in Congo to fight the Ebola outbreak are quarantining at a new isolation facility in ⁠Kenya after the US government introduced travel ⁠restrictions, the head of a US charity ​employing them told Reuters.

The aid workers are the first known people to quarantine at the facility, which has sparked huge opposition in Kenya and is at the heart of a legal case in which a court has ordered the work to be ⁠suspended. Construction continued, however, according to US officials and satellite imagery reviewed by Reuters.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Reuters

item.title: Sperm whales change ‘vowel’ sounds when boats are nearby, scientists discover
item.description:
Researchers listening off the Caribbean island of Dominica found variations in the mammals’ audible clicks when marine traffic was close

Sperm whales produce a specific pattern of clicking noises when they hear the noise of boats, researchers have found. The pattern is so distinct that the scientists who identified it said they can now use it to predict when ships are nearby.

Researchers from Project Ceti (the Cetacean Translation Initiative), a non-profit research organisation working to decode sperm whale communication, spent four years tuning in on the calls of 15 sperm whales off the coast of Dominica.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Emma Bryce

item.title: Trump announces new tariffs on generic drugs to take effect in 2028 – as it happened
item.description:
This live blog is now closed.

  • Sign up for US Breaking News email

US gas prices have continued to rise since the conflict with Iran resumed this month. According to the American Automobile Association (AAA), a gallon of fuel is now just over $4 – almost a dollar more than a year ago.

It comes as Brent Crude, the international oil benchmark, is now up by almost 2% to $90.92 a barrel.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Robert Mackey, Shrai Popat, Maya Yang and Tom Ambrose

item.title: Canada cancels joint bridge-opening celebration with US amid trade tensions
item.description:
The ribbon-cutting celebration of the Gordie Howe International Bridge is no longer taking place after Trump announced a 50% tariff on Canadian goods

A planned joint US-Canada ceremony Friday celebrating the opening of the Gordie Howe International Bridge connecting Detroit and Windsor, Ontario, is no longer expected to take place after Donald Trump announced a 50% tariff on most Canadian goods.

In announcing the tariffs, the president accused Canada of unfairly discriminating against US automobiles, alcohol and dairy products, escalating tensions with one of the United States’ closest allies.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Associated Press

item.title: Spanish hotel chain ceases operations in Cuba under pressure from Washington
item.description:
Meliá group says Donald Trump’s sanctions make it ‘impossible’ to keep any of its 34 sites open

A Spanish hotel chain is to cease all operations in Cuba at the end of the week due to “major difficulties” in doing business on the island under growing pressure from Washington.

The latest US sanctions make it “impossible, de facto and de jure, to maintain even minimal operational stability”, the Meliá group explained in a statement on Tuesday to the Spanish stock market regulator.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Agence France-Presse

item.title: US claims of Cuba’s ‘unique threat’ jar with reality of hunger and blackouts
item.description:
State department’s 100-page report is latest push in Trump administration campaign to bring down government

The US government’s latest allegations that Cuba is behind “many of the most significant upheavals in recent American political history” and that its leaders pose “a unique threat to American homeland security” have come as startling revelations to those living under the six-month-long US oil blockade in Havana, where desperate people search through piles of uncollected rubbish for food, or else swelter at home in 24-hour-plus blackouts.

The unsigned 100-page report, Cuba: Capital of 21st-Century Communism, published by the US state department on Monday, blames Havana for everything from “the George Floyd riots to the rise of antifa to the explosion of pro-terrorist activism on American college campuses”.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Ruaridh Nicoll in Havana

item.title: British woman jailed in Hong Kong after making false rape allegations
item.description:
Isabel Rose, 26, given six-year sentence for blackmail and perverting course of justice after accusing British banker

A British woman who reported being raped to police in Hong Kong has been sentenced to six years in prison for blackmail and perverting the course of public justice.

Isabel Rose, 26, from south-east London, travelled to Hong Kong in early 2024 to visit a British banker she had met while travelling in south-east Asia.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Danny Vincent

item.title: Australia to warn China it won’t be bullied by ‘provocative‘ actions as it builds military and nuclear arsenal
item.description:
Foreign minister Penny Wong will deliver speech criticising China’s recent launch of a nuclear-capable submarine-launched missile

  • Get our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcast

Penny Wong will warn China that Australia and other neighbouring nations will not be bullied by its “provocative” actions, after its involvement in another “destabilising and dangerous” incident this week.

The foreign affairs minister – who will deliver the address on Thursday in the Philippines, where the 59th Asean foreign minister’s meeting is being held – will also caution world leaders against placing a toll on the strait of Hormuz after both Iran and the US threatened to do amid the protracted conflict.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Sarah Basford Canales and Luca Ittimani

item.title: Michelin-starred restaurant owner in South Korea faces jail over ant dessert
item.description:
Prosecutors seek a one-year sentence and heavy fine after chef garnished dish with insects not approved for consumption

Prosecutors in South Korea have asked a court to sentence the owner of a Michelin-starred restaurant to one year in prison for serving a dessert topped with ants, an insect not approved for consumption under local food safety law.

The request, made on Monday, also sought a 20m won (£10,108) fine for the company that runs the restaurant, Yonhap news agency reported.

Continue reading...

media:content.media:credit: Photograph: Ministry of Food and Drug Safety Korea
Photograph: Ministry of Food and Drug Safety Korea
Photograph: Ministry of Food and Drug Safety Korea

item.dc:creator: Raphael Rashid

item.title: Japan records its first ‘cruelly hot day’ as cities swelter in 40-degree heat
item.description:
In April Japan coined new term kokushobi for days above 40C as country advises workers to wear shorts to the office to cope with stifling heat

Japan logged its first official kokushobi or “cruelly hot day” on Tuesday since the new term for when temperatures top 40 degrees Celsius was introduced in April. The Japan Meteorological Agency coined the new designation to warn the public of the heat dangers of days that were once freak weather events but have now become more common.

The central Japan cities of Gujo and Tajimi in Gifu prefecture, along with Toyota in Aichi prefecture, all hit 40C. Gujo’s 40C and Toyota’s 40.1C were all-time highs recorded at their respective weather stations.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Gavin Blair in Tokyo

item.title: Australia spent more than $9m for each asylum seeker held on Nauru last year – so why can’t they afford to buy food?
item.description:
Many of the more than 100 people awaiting processing on the tiny Pacific island are skipping meals to make ends meet, a new survey has found

  • Follow our Australia news live blog for latest updates

  • Get our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcast

Most refugees and asylum seekers held in Nauru’s detention centres say they cannot afford to buy enough food or access clean drinking water, despite the Australian government spending the equivalent of more than $9m per person last financial year.

The group of more than 100 people have been sent indefinitely to the tiny Pacific island while they wait for their asylum claims to be processed. Many of the cohort say they are skipping meals to make ends meet, according to a new survey by the Asylum Seeker Resource Centre (ASRC) advocacy group.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Sarah Basford Canales

item.title: ALP announces sweeping changes to student work placements as Gaza war looms large at party conference
item.description:
Anthony Albanese will cast ‘practical’ policies as the key to countering the anxiety and frustration in Australia

  • Get our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcast

Students in 10 fields including physiotherapy, speech pathology and clinical psychology will receive financial assistance to complete mandatory placements under a major expansion of the federal government’s paid work experience program.

The prime minister, Anthony Albanese, will make the announcement in a speech to open the Labor national conference on Thursday, which will cast “practical” policies as the key to combating the anxiety and frustration gripping the electorate.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Dan Jervis-Bardy Chief political correspondent

item.title: Court win for low-paid Domino’s workers in underpayment class action – as it happened
item.description:
This blog is now closed

  • Get our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcast

The New South Wales premier, Chris Minns, says the state’s Labor party is under threat from One Nation and “we’ve got to fight for those votes”.

Appearing on ABC Radio National this morning, Minns was asked if One Nation was a “racist party”. He said people who had concerns about the levels of immigration weren’t necessarily racist but some of the things Pauline Hanson had said were.

Certainly some of the things Pauline Hanson says are definitely racist, when she said there’s no such thing as a good Muslim. I mean, how else do you characterise that? You’ve got to be straight with people. And that clearly is. But I don’t think everyone who’s supporting them currently is racist at all … And we’ve got to fight for those votes.

I think that we are under threat in a whole bunch of seats as a result of the surge of votes for One Nation … So we’re doing some what we regard as practical commonsense changes to policy in New South Wales.

When people join the Do Not Call Register or unsubscribe from marketing messages, they are making a clear choice. Those choices must be respected – especially given the heightened risks of financial loss and psychological harm from gambling marketing.

The scale and range of these breaches point to serious weaknesses in TAB’s compliance systems.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Achol Arok and Caitlin Cassidy

item.title: People with secret ‘safe phones’ advised to turn off devices during national test of AusAlert emergency warning
item.description:
Ten-second ‘siren’ to blare on most phones on Monday as part of national test despite reservations from some states

  • Follow our Australia news live blog for latest updates

  • Get our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcast

An unsilenceable siren will blare out of most phones in Australia on Monday afternoon for 10 seconds, with the federal government confirming a national test of the new AusAlert emergency alert system will go ahead, despite reservations from some state governments.

Domestic violence campaigners and the National Emergency Management Agency have also repeated advice that people who may have secret “safe phones”, kept hidden for personal safety reasons, may want to turn off their devices for up to 24 hours after the test scheduled for 2pm (AEST) on Monday.

iPhones 11 and up running iOS 26.4 or later;

Apple Watch SE2, SE3, Series 6 and up running watchOS 26.4 or later;

Android devices running Android 12 or later.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Josh Butler

item.title: Brittany Higgins accuses Pauline Hanson of victim-blaming for calling domestic violence a ‘two-way street’
item.description:
One Nation leader criticised by advocates after telling women being abused: ‘don’t put up with it’

  • Follow our Australia news live blog for latest updates

  • Get our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcast

Pauline Hanson has described domestic violence as “wrong” but a “two-way street”, and that women being abused should not put up with it, drawing condemnation from advocates who have accused the One Nation leader of victim-blaming.

On Wednesday, Hanson told radio station 4BC domestic violence in Australia was a scourge but that society was under “so much pressure”, when asked to call out the issue.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Krishani Dhanji

item.title: Alleged rape victim ‘could have done a lot better’ if she had made up claims against well-connected Melbourne man, court told
item.description:
Prosecutor tells court that ‘gaps or inconsistencies’ in the woman’s account ‘enhances her credibility’, but the defence maintains she is lying

  • Follow our Australia news live blog for latest updates

  • Get our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcast

A woman who accused a well-connected Melbourne man of rape “could have done a much better job” if she was inventing the story for money, a Melbourne court has heard.

But the accused’s lawyer has described the prosecution’s case as a “horrendous mixture” of “lies, fabrications, inconsistencies, [and] evidence that has been found to be demonstrably untrue”.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Nino Bucci Justice and courts reporter

item.title: Norway’s national oil company’s profits double to $11.5bn amid war on Iran
item.description:
Equinor benefits from move to increase oil and gas production during strait of Hormuz blockades

  • Business live – latest updates

Profits at Norway’s state oil company nearly doubled to $11.5bn (£8.6bn) in the three months to the end of June as the jump in oil and gas prices caused by the war against Iran boosted earnings.

Equinor– which is the UK’s biggest gas supplier – benefited from a decision to increase oil and gas production at the start of the conflict, filling a gap in the market after a near-halt to shipping through the strait of Hormuz caused Gulf oil flows to slump.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Kalyeena Makortoff

item.title: EU bids to finalise new Russia sanctions amid discontent from some member states – Europe live
item.description:
Brussels talks between senior diplomats now underway in attempt to sign off 21st package of sanctions, after previous failed attempts

The Venice Biennale said it would challenge a European Union decision to withdraw a €2 ⁠million grant in response to the return of Russia to its 2026 edition, despite continuing aggression on Ukraine, Reuters reported.

The Italian government sharply criticised the move, while the European Commission warned it ​would suspend or terminate funding if the Russian ‌pavilion returned.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Jakub Krupa

item.title: ​World’s largest meat company faces legal challenge over green credentials of $6bn global expansion
item.description:
Greenpeace petitions Dutch courts to order JBS to disclose how its aims are compatible with responsibilities to climate and human rights

The world’s biggest meat company, JBS, faces a landmark legal challenge to disclose how its $6bn (£4.5bn) global expansion plan is compatible with responsibilities to the climate, nature and human rights.

Courts in the Netherlands, where JBS is headquartered, will assess the petition filed by Greenpeace for information on whether the company is compliant with Dutch laws on “duty of care” as well as its own publicly stated commitments.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Watts

item.title: Zelenskyy fires Ukraine’s army chief and offers Fedorov ‘prominent position’
item.description:
After nationwide protests Oleksandr Syrskyi is to be replaced in reshuffle and popular defence minister returns to government

Volodymyr Zelenskyy has fired Ukraine’s commander-in-chief Oleksandr Syrskyi and offered a “prominent position” to the defence minister he sacked last week in a move that sparked nationwide protests.

In a late-night TV address, Zelenskyy said he was appointing Mykhailo Drapatyi, a popular and innovative general, as the new head of Ukraine’s armed forces. Drapatyi would oversee a reorganisation of the army’s command structure, Ukraine’s president said.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Luke Harding in Dnipro

item.title: France becomes first EU country to ban social media access for under-15s
item.description:
Parliament votes to stop children using apps such as TikTok in policy championed by Emmanuel Macron

France’s parliament has approved a bill banning social media access for children under 15, making it the first EU country to bar children from apps such as TikTok.

The president, Emmanuel Macron, has championed the ban as a key reform of his final term in office and pledged to enforce it by September.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: AFP

item.title: US renews strikes on Iran as Tehran claims no nuclear activity at site threatened by Trump – Middle East crisis live
item.description:
US military completes 11th night of strikes as attacks overshadow diplomatic efforts in Pakistan to salvage interim ceasefire deal

  • What is Pickaxe Mountain, Iran’s underground nuclear facility?

The Jordanian army said it intercepted four Iranian missiles on Wednesday with two additional missile falling in an open area, AFP reports.

The two missiles that the army did not intercept fell in “two remote, uninhabited areas, without causing any human casualties or material damage” the army said in a statement.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Vivian Ho (now) and Mark Saunokonoko (earlier)

item.title: Mamdani urges US government to arrest Netanyahu if Israeli PM visits US
item.description:
New York mayor admits he does not have authority to arrest Netanyahu, adding Israeli PM is ‘architect of a horrific genocide’

Zohran Mamdani, the New York City mayor, acknowledged on Tuesday he does not have the authority to arrest Benjamin Netanyahu, but urged US federal authorities to do so if the Israeli prime minister visits the US later this year.

“Benjamin Netanyahu is a war criminal, the architect of a horrific genocide against the Palestinian people,” Mamdani said in a video posted to social media.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Robert Mackey

item.title: Wednesday briefing: How Trump’s Iran gamble has produced a crisis reshaping the global order
item.description:
In today’s newsletter: ​A White House operating without expertise, strategy or restraint ​is stuck in a conflict with no easy way out, which is sending economic shockwaves far beyond its own borders

Good morning. Within two hours of entering No 10 on Monday afternoon, Andy Burnham had his first call with Donald Trump. After chit-chatting about the football (both agreed England had an “excellent tournament”) and Manchester (our new prime minister invited the president to visit the city Trump once described as a “town”), they got on to far trickier territory: the strait of Hormuz.

The US has attacked Iran for the eleventh consecutive night, hitting aircraft hangars and drone storage sites across the country. It came after Iran launched retaliatory strikes on US air bases and neighbouring Gulf states, which have accused it of deliberately targeting desalination plants that are essential to life in the region. Each side now claims it is preventing the other from gaining control over the strait of Hormuz, through which 20% of the world’s oil was shipped before the war began. Vessels carrying crucial shipments of oil, gas and fertiliser remain at a standstill.

UK politics | Andy Burnham is under mounting pressure to explain how he will pay for his policy pledges as he implored his new cabinet to examine “all possible ways” to tackle the cost of living crisis.

UK news | A man has appeared in court charged with the murder of the former MP and Reform UK spokesperson Ann Widdecombe. Joshua Kerry, 28, is accused of killing the former Conservative minister at her home in Haytor, Devon, by hitting her on the head with a hammer 21 times.

Middle East | The US defense secretary, Pete Hegseth, told the Senate the US-Israel war on Iran had cost $37.5bn so far, as he faced lawmakers furious over the conflict’s soaring price tag and the deaths last week of three American service members.

Climate crisis | About 23 million people will be banned from using hosepipes by this weekend, as England and Wales moves closer to drought status.

Ukraine | Volodymyr Zelenskyy has fired Ukraine’s commander-in-chief Oleksandr Syrskyi and offered a “prominent position” to the defence minister he sacked last week in a move that sparked nationwide protests.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Hettie O'Brien

item.title: Iran’s foreign minister describes moment of attack that killed Khamenei
item.description:
Abbas Araghchi says he has kept on the move since being pulled from rubble after strike on first day of war

Iran’s foreign minister, Abbas Araghchi, has described how he was pulled from the rubble of a leadership compound after the US-Israeli airstrike that killed the country’s supreme leader, Ali Khamenei, on the first day of the war, and has survived since by constantly moving around the capital, Tehran, by car.

In an interview with the Iranian journalist Javad Mogouyi, Araghchi confirmed Israel and the US had accurate intelligence on the location of Khamenei and other officials, enabling them to target Iran’s senior leadership.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Patrick Wintour Diplomatic editor

item.title: Hegseth estimates rising cost of Iran war now at $37.5bn – as it happened
item.description:
This blog is now closed. Read our latest full report here.

Lebanon’s president, Joseph Aoun, is due to meet Donald Trump for the first time today as he wraps up a four-day visit to Washington.

The meeting coincides with ongoing US-mediated talks between Lebanon and Israel. The two sides announced a “framework agreement” on 26 June which stipulates the withdrawal of Israeli forces from southern Lebanon as well as the disarmament of Hezbollah.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Mark Saunokonoko, Maya Yang, Nadeem Badshah, Tom Ambrose, Taz Ali and Adam Fulton

item.title: Opposition parties shut down Indian parliament in solidarity with ‘cockroach’ protests
item.description:
Proceedings suspended as politicians call for Modi government to address police violence and education scandals

India’s opposition parties have shut down parliament in a protest calling for the resignation of the education minister, in solidarity with the new youth-led “cockroach” movement.

Opposition politicians, led by the Congress party leader Rahul Gandhi, gathered outside the parliament building in Delhi on Wednesday, many dressed in black and waving placards calling on Dharmendra Pradhan to quit.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Hannah Ellis-Petersen in Delhi

item.title: India’s Cockroach movement protesters show no sign of backing down
item.description:
Thousands of people again on the streets of Delhi calling for education reforms despite police violence on Monday

Taru had come to the protest prepared this time. In one hand was an anti-government poster and in the other was a bicycle helmet. “We need to have protection in case Delhi police beat us up again,” said the 25-year-old, who stood among her friends. “We are not scared of them and we won’t run away, no matter what they do. We’ll stay here till they listen.”

On Tuesday, central Delhi still thronged with thousands of protesters, as an unprecedented expression of dissent against the powerful ruling Narendra Modi government showed no sign of abating.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Hannah Ellis-Petersen in Delhi

item.title: Rescuers in Afghanistan search for missing and dead after flash floods destroy homes
item.description:
National disaster agency confirms ‘severe financial and human losses’ in eastern province of Nuristan

Rescue crews in Afghanistan are searching for survivors and bodies among the rubble of houses destroyed by devastating flash floods that authorities said had killed nearly two dozen people and left more than 100 people missing.

The Afghanistan national disaster management authority said on Tuesday that 23 people had died, 80 had been injured and more than 100 were missing after floods struck the eastern province of Nuristan on Monday, causing an undetermined number of buildings to collapse. The provincial capital city of Parun was among the areas affected, authorities said.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Associated Press in Kabul

item.title: Tens of thousands of Cockroach movement protesters defy police with Delhi march
item.description:
Youth-led group calls on education minister to quit in one of India’s largest anti-government rallies in years

Tens of thousands of protesters have defied police orders and descended on Delhi to demand the resignation of India’s education minister in one of the largest anti-government demonstrations in years.

A huge crowd, largely made up of students and gen Z-ers, filled the streets in the heart of India’s capital from early Monday morning shouting anti-government slogans. Hundreds had already camped out overnight.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Hannah Ellis-Petersen in Delhi

item.title: ‘When I get home, my whole body aches’: extreme heat saps health and wages of India’s poorest workers
item.description:
Exclusive: Outdoor workers losing up to 9% of income as weather becomes ‘poverty multiplier’, research says

Extreme heat is pushing some of India’s poorest workers further into poverty, with income losses of up to 9% for outdoor workers, new research has found.

Analysis done by the International Institute for Environment and Development (IIED), and shared exclusively with the Guardian, found that extreme heat is acting as a “poverty multiplier” for hundreds of millions of workers in India’s informal sector.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Carmen Aguilar García and Antonio Voce in London; Hannah Ellis-Petersen and Aakash Hassan in Delhi

item.title: Former Southern Water employees to face trial over alleged cover-up in wastewater testing
item.description:
Ex-chief executive Matthew Wright among those named in criminal proceedings by Environment Agency

Britain’s ⁠Environment Agency (EA) is ⁠bringing ​a fraud case against ⁠four former Southern Water employees, including ⁠the former chief executive ​Matthew ‌Wright, over ‌allegations they manipulated wastewater ‌testing results.

The regulator said the four had allegedly conspired ‌to defraud the Environment Agency and ​water regulator Ofwat between 2012 and 2017 by ⁠creating artificial “no-flow” ​events ​at wastewater treatment ​works to ​influence ‌compliance checks.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Reuters

item.title: Burnham to review early prisoner release scheme amid anger over potential freeing of PC Harper’s killers – UK politics live
item.description:
New PM says the main perpetrator in the killing of PC Andrew Harper will now ‘not be subject to early release’ and he will also review ‘whole policy’

Wes Streeting said the US president was “right” to demand Europe pays more for its own defence.

Donald Trump has been increasingly critical of Europe for relying too much on the US for its security, and has demanded Nato allies reach 5% of GDP on defence spending by 2035.

President Trump has called on Europe to carry a greater share of the burden for our own defence, and he is right to do so. The UK has always been a leading light on defence spending, but the world is changing and in these dangerous uncertain times it is clear that we have needed to go further.

That’s why we’ve committed £298bn over the next four years, including an additional £15bn on top of last year’s spending review settlement. At the recent Nato summit, all members reaffirmed our commitment to 3.5 by 35 [3.5% of GDP by 2035].

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Tom Ambrose (now) and Taz Ali (earlier)

item.title: NHS trust investigated for corporate manslaughter after Nottingham attacks
item.description:
Trust that treated Valdo Calocane, who killed three people in 2023, sectioned him four times between 2020 and 2022

The NHS trust that treated the triple killer Valdo Calocane in the years before the Nottingham attacks is being investigated by police for corporate manslaughter.

Calocane, who has paranoid schizophrenia, was sentenced to an indefinite hospital order after he fatally stabbed the 19-year-old students Barnaby Webber and Grace O’Malley Kumar, and the 65-year-old caretaker Ian Coates, on 13 June 2023.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Neha Gohil Midlands correspondent

item.title: Jessica Morgan named new Tate director – with ‘big pay cut‘ to take job
item.description:
Clear frontrunner leaves lucrative role at New York’s Dia Art Foundation to join UK arts organisation at critical juncture

Jessica Morgan has been confirmed as the new director of Tate, marking her return to the arts organisation where she worked as a curator for more than a decade, at a critical juncture in its history.

Morgan, the clear frontrunner in the race to replace Maria Balshaw, who left this spring after nine years in charge, is currently based in New York, where she has led the Dia Art Foundation since 2015.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Lanre Bakare Arts and culture correspondent

item.title: Andy Burnham announces review of prisoner early release scheme
item.description:
PM says he wants to be sure everything concerning controversial policy has been looked at before it goes ahead

  • UK politics live – latest updates

Andy Burnham has said he will review the government’s controversial prisoner early release scheme with the justice secretary.

The scheme, under which thousands of prisoners are to serve shorter sentences after new laws were passed to address a prison overcrowding crisis, is due to begin in September.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Topping Political correspondent

item.title: Trump ally pledges to ‘work with people’ he disagrees with after Arizona primary win – US politics live
item.description:
Andy Biggs – a hard-right congressman endorsed by Donald Trump – won the Republican nomination for Arizona governor

  • Sign up for the Breaking News US email

Also today, we’ll hear from US trade representative Jamieson Greer, when he testifies in front of senators on the finance committee at 10am ET.

This comes just days after Donald Trump announced 50% tariffs on most Canadian goods, leading to fresh tensions and more negotiations with Ottawa, ahead of the duties taking effect next month.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Shrai Popat (now) and Tom Ambrose (earlier)

item.title: Trump administration says 15 agencies will get $5bn in ‘AI for science’ effort
item.description:
Administration will also overhaul how US government funds federal research by supporting individual scientists and AI over universities

The US will spend $5bn to tackle longstanding scientific problems across multiple fields using AI, ⁠the Trump administration said in a statement on Wednesday. The agencies will use the funding to identify the root causes ⁠of chronic diseases, accelerate drug ⁠discovery and ​develop longer-lasting building materials, among other tasks, according to the statement.

Scientists will have access to the Department of Energy’s supercomputers, AI and specialized ⁠datasets, along with other components needed to run experiments using algorithms, said Michael Kratsios, chief technology adviser to Donald Trump, in an ⁠interview with Reuters.

Continue reading...

media:content.media:credit: Photograph: Patrícia de Melo Moreira/AFP/Getty Images
Photograph: Patrícia de Melo Moreira/AFP/Getty Images
Photograph: Patrícia de Melo Moreira/AFP/Getty Images

item.dc:creator: Reuters

item.title: $37.5bn cost of Iran war revealed as senator warns of ‘another forever war’ | First Thing
item.description:
Pete Hegseth reveals costs so far as Trump administration seeks more funding. Plus, rediscovered plane wreck that changed flying for ever

  • Don’t already get First Thing in your inbox? Sign up here

Good morning.

The US defense secretary, Pete Hegseth, has told the Senate that the US-Israel war on Iran has cost $37.5bn so far, as he faced lawmakers furious over the conflict’s soaring price tag and the deaths last week of three American service members. He defended the Trump administration’s roughly $88bn supplemental funding request, calling it “urgent, necessary”.

What is happening in Iran? The Iranian foreign minister, Abbas Araghchi, has described how he was pulled from the rubble of a leadership compound after the US-Israeli airstrike that killed the country’s supreme leader, Ali Khamenei, on the first day of the war, and has survived since by constantly moving around Tehran by car. Deepa Parent, meanwhile, has reports from the ground with ordinary Iranians, who say “it’s truly hell here” as airstrikes, repression and water shortages become the “new normal”.

What other developments have affected the region? Zohran Mamdani, New York’s mayor, has acknowledged he does not have the authority to arrest Benjamin Netanyahu, but urged US federal authorities to do so if the Israeli prime minister visits the US later this year. The White House has announced that American airline carriers will be able to resume direct flights to Lebanon more than 40 years after the US suspended the route.

What is uranium enrichment? It is the process of increasing the presence of isotope uranium-235 so the substance can sustain a nuclear chain reaction. Enrichment is not an automatic path to a nuclear weapon, but could open the door to a Saudi Arabia weapons programme, something its de facto leader, Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman, has not ruled out if Iran obtains an atomic bomb.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Martin Belam

item.title: Six beluga whales finally leave embattled Canadian marine park
item.description:
Whales flown to US aquariums after long-running saga in which Marineland had threatened to euthanise them

Six beluga whales have at last left Marineland, the embattled Canadian marine park in Niagara Falls that previously threatened to kill its own animals, and are arriving at US aquariums this week.

While the whales’ journey into the US marks the end of their long and controversial saga of captivity in Canada, advocates say the new conditions, though superior to Marineland, could bring continued challenges.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Olivia Bowden in Toronto

item.title: ‘Our hearts are crushed’: Hannah Rapp’s violent death sparks outpouring of grief
item.description:
Family and boxing community grieving deeply after Rapp was killed by motorist while she was cycling in Texas

The violent death of the US boxer Hannah Rapp, whom authorities say was killed recently by a motorist while she cycled, has left her sport, her communities outside the ring and her loved ones grieving deeply just weeks after she took her first world title shot.

A statement from the Bryan Boxing gym where Rapp trained in Texas commemorated her as “an amazing lady and soul” while the Aggieland Cycling club to which she belonged recalled how her “kindness and excitement for life [buoyed] all who knew her”.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Ramon Antonio Vargas
